name: Vedanta Deshika
dates_notes: Sectarian chronicles plus manuscript colophons; dates well-established.

dates_evidence.description: Śrīvaiṣṇava sectarian chronicles document his birth, education, and demise dates.
Manuscript colophons across his many works confirm his floruit and lineage.

lineage_polemical.note: The Vaḍakalai-Tenkalai split formally crystallizes around Vedānta Deśika's monkey-hold (markaṭa-nyāya) account vs. Pillai-Lokācārya's cat-hold (mārjāra-nyāya).

Vedānta Deśika's Śatadūṣaṇī engages the tathāgatagarbha doctrine (transmitted into the Indian polemical milieu primarily through the Laṅkāvatāra-Sūtra and the Ratna-Gotra-Vibhāga transmission) in the course of refuting the Advaita identity-thesis: any doctrine of universal Buddha-nature, like any doctrine of universal Brahman-identity, fails to preserve the asymmetric paratantra-svatantra dependence-relation the Vedāntic realist holds essential.

Vedānta-Deśika's Pāñcarātra-Rakṣā is the most extensive late-medieval defence of Pāñcarātra authority, addressing every objection raised by Mīmāṃsakas and Advaitins to the Saṃhitās' status as pramāṇa, and is the canonical Vaḍakalai handbook for Pāñcarātra ritual-theology.

core_thesis: Vedānta Deśika is the systematiser, logician, and Vaḍakalai polemicist of post-Rāmānuja Viśiṣṭādvaita (Clooney's *Beyond Compare*, 2008, is the standard English-language secondary engagement with Deśika's *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra*, paired with Francis de Sales' *Treatise on the Love of God*; the work is not on disk in our corpus, cited via title-thesis and known scholarly summary). The architecture is preserved without revision — Brahman as Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī, qualified by *cit* and *acit* as his body in *apṛthak-siddha* relation — and extended on three fronts. First, the engagement with Navya-Nyāya. The *Nyāya-Pariśuddhi* and *Nyāya-Siddhāñjana* build a Viśiṣṭādvaita-compatible *padārtha*-scheme and *pramāṇa*-theory at the technical level of Gaṅgeśa's school: three *pramāṇa*-s (*pratyakṣa*, *anumāna*, *śabda*), with *upamāna* and *arthāpatti* reduced to *anumāna* and *anupalabdhi* rejected as redundant; *yathārtha-khyāti* defended against the *anyathā-* and *anirvacanīya-khyāti* alternatives; *apṛthak-siddhi* analytically distinguished from both *samavāya* (inherence) and *saṃyoga* (conjunction). Second, the *Śatadūṣaṇī* — one hundred numbered *dūṣaṇa*-s against post-Śaṅkara Advaita as developed by Maṇḍana, Sureśvara, Vācaspati, Padmapāda, Prakāśātman, Sarvajñātman, and Śrīharṣa. The polemic extends Rāmānuja's *sapta-vidhānupapatti* but operates argument-by-argument rather than thematically: *anirvacanīya-khyāti* is dismantled in *dūṣaṇa* form by exhibiting that an indeterminable category cannot underwrite a determinate explanation of error; *dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi* by exhibiting that the supposed self-creating cognition presupposes the very world-distinctions it is meant to generate; Śrīharṣa's anti-definitional *Khaṇḍana* by re-asserting that any dialectical exchange — including Śrīharṣa's own — presupposes a *padārtha*-scheme it cannot consistently deny. Third, on the *upāya*-question Deśika defends the Vaḍakalai position. *Bhakti-yoga* requires the surrendered self's continuing effort and qualifications, and *prapatti* itself is structured by the six *aṅga*-s of the *Śaraṇāgati-Gadya* as conditions the agent must enact (*Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* *adhikāra* 9, *aṅga-prakaraṇa*); grace cooperates with the agent's continuing co-action (*Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* *adhikāra* 14, *kartṛtva-anumati-prakaraṇa*). The image is the *markaṭa-nyāya* — the monkey-baby that grips its mother — against the Tenkalai *mārjāra-nyāya* of the kitten the mother cat carries (the Vaḍakalai-Tenkalai contrast condensed in the *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* *adhikāra*-s 22–24). Sanskritic Vedic learning is retained and defended as essential to the school's competence.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Mukta-Kalapa
engaged_works.summary: A 500-verse Sanskrit *kārikā*-treatise organised by the Viśiṣṭādvaita ontology of *dravya* and *adravya* into *sara*-chapters: *jaḍa-dravya-sara* (on matter), *jīva-sara* (on the self), *nitya-sara* (on the eternal *vibhūti*), *Īśvara-sara* (on the Lord), *buddhi-sara* (on *dharma-bhūta-jñāna*). The treatise is the school's mature systematic ontology in compressed verse form. Each Viśiṣṭādvaita category is fixed by argued contrast with the rival schemes — Sāṃkhya *prakṛti*, Naiyāyika *paramāṇu*-atomism, Buddhist momentariness, Advaitic *māyā*, Bhāskara's *aupādhika-bheda*, Madhva's unqualified *bheda* — and the contrasts work by exhibiting where each rival category-scheme misdescribes the data. Buddhist momentariness fails on continuant-recognition; Advaitic *māyā* on the self-luminosity argument; Madhva's pure *bheda* on the *antaryāmin*-passages. The compressed verse form leaves the dialectical detail to the auto-commentary *Sarvārtha-Siddhi*. The work is the load-bearing source for Vaḍakalai metaphysics in the verse-doctrinal register and is read together with the *Sarvārtha-Siddhi* in modern pāṭhaśālā instruction.

engaged_works.title: Sarvartha-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Sanskrit prose auto-commentary on the Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus; manuscript transmission inseparable from Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa.

engaged_works.summary: Vedānta-Deśika's own Sanskrit prose auto-commentary on the *Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa*. Where the verse-treatise compresses the school's ontology into 500 *kārikās*, the *Sarvārtha-Siddhi* unfolds the dialectic at length: each *kārikā* receives a *pūrvapakṣa*-*siddhānta* exposition with the rival schemes argued against in their own technical idiom. The auto-commentary is unusually rigorous on two points. First, *dharmi-bhūta-jñāna* as the *jīva*'s constitutive attribute — substance-awareness, not awareness *simpliciter* — and the implications for the school's account of cognition, error, and *jīvanmukti*. Second, *apṛthak-siddhi* as a sui generis ontological relation distinct from both *samavāya* (inherence, which would dissolve the relata) and *saṃyoga* (conjunction, which would leave them externally related). The work matters because it is the canonical reference for Vaḍakalai metaphysics in the prose-dialectical register; later Viśiṣṭādvaita scholasticism — including Vīrarāghavācārya's *Sarvārtha-Siddhi-Vyākhyā* — cites the *Sarvārtha-Siddhi* on every contested category-boundary. The *Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa* and *Sarvārtha-Siddhi* are inseparable in manuscript transmission but are now distinguished here as separate work-entries to let later sub-commentaries cite the appropriate target.

engaged_works.title: Rahasya-Traya-Sara
engaged_works.summary: A long Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa rahasya-treatise in 32 *adhikāra*-s expounding the three Śrīvaiṣṇava esoteric *mantra*-s (Clooney's *Beyond Compare*, 2008, is the standard English-language secondary reading, said to work *adhikāra* by *adhikāra* through the *aṅga*-doctrine and the Vaḍakalai-Tenkalai contrast; the work is not on disk in our corpus, cited via title-thesis and known scholarly summary) — *tirumantra* (*aum namo nārāyaṇāya*), *dvaya*, *carama-śloka* — and through them the totality of Vaḍakalai soteriology. The treatise is the canonical Vaḍakalai handling of the *upāya*-question. Its load-bearing claims are four. First, *prapatti* (*śaraṇāgati*) is enjoined for those incapable of sustained *bhakti-yoga*, but it is not the only path; it is the path for the *ākiñcana*. Second, *prapatti* is structured by the six *aṅga*-s of Rāmānuja's *Śaraṇāgati-Gadya* — *ānukūlya-saṅkalpa*, *prātikūlya-varjana*, *rakṣiṣyatīti viśvāsa*, *goptṛtva-varaṇa*, *ātma-nikṣepa*, *kārpaṇya* — read as conditions the surrendered self must satisfy, not merely as descriptions of an already-given state. Third, the *jīva* retains real agency (*kartṛtva*) under the Lord's permission (*anumati*); grace presupposes and cooperates with the agent's continuing co-action. Fourth, Śrī functions as *puruṣakāra* — mediating advocate enabling Nārāyaṇa's grace — and not as an independent *upāya* in concert with him. Each of these is the Vaḍakalai contention against the Tenkalai of Pillai-Lokācārya's *Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa*. Deśika discriminates *prapatti* from *bhakti-yoga* carefully, without making them rivals; the *markaṭa-nyāya* image (the monkey-baby that grips its mother) condenses the cooperative structure. Composed in Maṇipravāḷa to be accessible to non-Sanskritist Śrīvaiṣṇavas without ceding the field to the Tenkalai. Every later Vaḍakalai position on grace, agency, qualification, and the status of Śrī cites the *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* as authority.

engaged_works.title: Pancharatra-Raksha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored defense of Pāñcarātra ritual practice.
engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit polemical *prakaraṇa* defending the *prāmāṇya* of the Pāñcarātra *saṃhitā*-s and the legitimacy of Vaiṣṇava temple-ritual, extending Yāmuna's *Āgama-Prāmāṇya* against the Smārta and Advaita-aligned challenges that resurfaced in the late-medieval period. The decisive *Brahma-Sūtra* terrain is the *Utpatti-Asambhava-Adhikaraṇa* (2.2.42–45), on which the orthodoxy of Pāñcarātra hinges. Deśika argues *sūtra* by *sūtra* that the passage refutes one heterodox version of *vyūha*-doctrine — the view that Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, and Aniruddha are *jīva*-emanations, which would make a created entity creator — and not Pāñcarātra cosmology as such. The orthodox four-*vyūha* doctrine, on which all four are forms of the supreme Brahman *cāturvyūham paraṃ brahma ṣāḍguṇya-parikarmitam* (PR 1.maṅgalācaraṇa), survives the *sūtra*-objection intact. Two further moves complete the defence. The taxonomic move — that the Pāñcarātra corpus divides into *Āgama-*, *Mantra-*, *Tantra-*, and *Tantrāntara-siddhānta* (PR 1, *catvāraḥ siddhāntāḥ* section), as the Veda divides into *Ṛg* and the others — establishes the Pāñcarātra as an internally articulated, Veda-analogous canon rather than a free sectarian invention. The jurisdictional move — that the non-conflict and usefulness of both *śāstra*-s have led impartial authorities to assign each to its proper domain (PR 1, *avirodhopayogābhyām ubhayor api śāstrayoḥ*) — yields the institutional settlement that Vaikhānasa and Pāñcarātra both stand within Vaidika ritual order. The work secured the authority of the Śrīvaiṣṇava temple system — Śrīraṅgam, Tirupati, Kāñcī — at the moment when sectarian competition with Advaita-aligned Smārta institutions was intensifying.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Parishuddhi
engaged_works.summary: The school's mature engagement with Nyāya logic and epistemology and its definitive *prāmāṇya*-theory. Three *pramāṇa*-s are admitted — *pratyakṣa*, *anumāna*, *śabda*; *upamāna* and *arthāpatti* are reduced to *anumāna*, *anupalabdhi* rejected as redundant. The treatise defends *yathārtha-khyāti* (every cognition has a real object; error is partial apprehension, not the cognising of a non-entity) against both *anyathā-khyāti* and the Advaitic *anirvacanīya-khyāti*. *Śabda* is defended as *svataḥ-prāmāṇya* — its validity intrinsic to the cognition it generates rather than parasitic on subsequent verification. The treatment of *vyāpti* (invariable concomitance) and *parāmarśa* (the consideration that combines the universal connection with the particular case) is constructed to engage Gaṅgeśa's Navya-Nyāya at its own level of technical precision. The work matters because before Deśika the school had Yāmuna's *Saṃvit-Siddhi* and Rāmānuja's scattered epistemological remarks but no comprehensive *pramāṇa*-treatise; the *Nyāya-Pariśuddhi* fills the gap and gives Viśiṣṭādvaita the technical resources to argue on equal footing with the Naiyāyika and Advaita scholasticisms dominant in the late thirteenth and fourteenth centuries.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Siddhanjana
engaged_works.summary: A systematic Viśiṣṭādvaita *prakaraṇa* on the seven categories — *dravya* (substance), *guṇa* (quality), *karma* (action), *sāmānya* (universal), *viśeṣa* (particularity), *samavāya* (inherence), *abhāva* (absence) — re-engineered against the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika scheme to fit Viśiṣṭādvaita ontology. Substance divides into *jaḍa* (*prakṛti*, *kāla*, *śuddha-sattva* of the eternal realm) and *ajaḍa* (*jīva*, *Īśvara*, *dharma-bhūta-jñāna*). The *jīva* is the substrate of its own attribute-awareness *dharma-bhūta-jñāna*, which expands and contracts under *karma* but is in its essential nature infinite; in *mokṣa* it expands to encompass Brahman and the eternal realm without merging the *jīva* into Brahman. Deśika gives the school's most precise analytic statement of *apṛthak-siddhi* as a relation distinct from *samavāya* and from *saṃyoga*: inseparability without inherence, since the relata retain their distinct natures, and without mere conjunction, since the relation is constitutive rather than contingent. He also clarifies the *antaryāmin*-structure: *Īśvara* can be inner controller of *jīva*-s and *acit* because the *śarīra-śarīrin* relation does not require numerical identity, and is not refuted by the persistent distinction of inner-self from body. The work complements the *Tattva-Mukta-Kalāpa* as the school's mature *padārtha*-systematisation; it supplies the technical vocabulary used by Sudarśana Sūri's *Śruta-Prakāśikā* and the later commentarial tradition.

engaged_works.title: Shata-Dushani
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored anti-Advaita polemic in 100 dūṣaṇa-s.
engaged_works.summary: One hundred numbered *dūṣaṇa*-s against post-Śaṅkara Advaita — the most systematic anti-Advaita polemic the Viśiṣṭādvaita tradition possesses. The targets span the school as developed by Maṇḍana, Sureśvara, Vācaspati, Padmapāda, Prakāśātman, Sarvajñātman, and Śrīharṣa, and the operation is argument-by-argument rather than thematic: each *dūṣaṇa* takes one Advaitic thesis at one of its precise formulations and exhibits its incoherence. Among the load-bearing engagements: the doctrine of *avidyā* (extending and sharpening Rāmānuja's *sapta-vidhānupapatti* across multiple *dūṣaṇa*-s, with separate refutations of the *Bhāmatī* locus-in-*jīva* and *Vivaraṇa* locus-in-Brahman positions); *anirvacanīya-khyāti* (an indeterminable category cannot underwrite a determinate explanation of error); *dṛṣṭi-sṛṣṭi* and *sṛṣṭi-dṛṣṭi* cosmologies (a self-creating cognition presupposes the world-distinctions it is meant to generate); *nirviśeṣa* Brahman (no cognition is of an unqualified content, so the supposed cognition of unqualified Brahman is not a cognition); the Advaitic readings of *tat tvam asi* and *neti neti*; the *bimba-pratibimba* and *avaccheda* models of the *jīva*; the late-Advaita *māyā/avidyā* distinction; the use of *jahad-ajahad-lakṣaṇā*; and Śrīharṣa's *Khaṇḍana*-style anti-definitional moves. Against Śrīharṣa, Deśika's counter-move is that any dialectical exchange — including Śrīharṣa's own — presupposes a coherent *padārtha*-scheme it cannot consistently deny. The work is the high-water mark of Vaḍakalai polemic; every later Viśiṣṭādvaita engagement with Advaita — Mahācārya, Śrīnivāsadāsa, and into the modern period — operates within the framework of objections the *Śatadūṣaṇī* established. Deśika did not complete the planned hundred *dūṣaṇa*-s in his lifetime; the tradition completed the count.

engaged_works.title: Adhikarana-Saravali
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Deśika's mature period; companion to the Tattva-Mukta-Kalāpa as a Brahma-Sūtra-organized doctrinal summary.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored verse-summary of the Brahma-Sūtra adhikaraṇas in Vaḍakalai Viśiṣṭādvaita perspective.
engaged_works.summary: A verse-summary (*sārāvalī*, "flow of essence") of the *Brahma-Sūtra*'s 156 *adhikaraṇa*-s, organised sequentially through the four *adhyāya*-s and giving each *adhikaraṇa* the Vaḍakalai Viśiṣṭādvaita reading in compressed *anuṣṭubh* form. For each *adhikaraṇa* Deśika supplies the standard four moves: *viṣaya* (topic), *saṃśaya* (doubt), *pūrvapakṣa* (typically Advaita or Bhāskara), *siddhānta* (the Rāmānuja-line conclusion as developed through Sudarśana Sūri's *Śruta-Prakāśikā*), and *prayojana* (purpose). The work is the canonical Vaḍakalai pedagogical summary of the entire *Brahma-Sūtra* — what every Vaḍakalai pandit has memorised and what every Vaḍakalai composition presupposes. Three doctrinally distinctive readings are worth marking. The *ānanda-mayādhikaraṇa* takes *ānandamaya* as Brahman, against Śaṅkara's reading on which it is the inner *kośa*. The *jīva-pratibimbādhikaraṇa* fixes the *apṛthak-siddha* relation against both the Advaitic *avaccheda* (the *jīva* as Brahman delimited by *upādhi*) and Bhāskara's *aupādhika-bheda* (difference itself as conditional). The *adhyāya*-4 *bheda-sūtra*-s are read in a *taratamya* key consistent with Viśiṣṭādvaita's *jīva*-individuation, against the Advaitic reduction of all *jīva*-difference to the *upādhi*-level. The work is the natural counterpart to the *Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa*, reorganising the same doctrinal architecture along the *Sūtra*'s own *adhikaraṇa*-skeleton.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
cāturvyūhaṃ paraṃ brahma ṣāḍguṇya-parikarmitam |
pañcarātrasya kṛtsnasya prasūtiṃ paryupāsmahe ||

samasa_vigrahas.note: describes Brahman as manifested in the four vyūhas
qualified by the classical six divine excellences
traditional title of the āgama corpus

karaka_structure.note: appositional accusative: the source/origin of the Pāñcarātra as a whole

verb_modality.note: 1st person plural present indicative with devotional-reflexive force

key_passages.english_close: [He is] the supreme Brahman, [manifested] as the four vyūhas, and adorned with the six divine excellences (ṣāḍguṇya). The source of the whole Pāñcarātra — him we reverentially worship.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (emanational form of Viṣṇu such as Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha)
(the six divine attributes, especially jñāna, bala, aiśvarya, śakti, vīrya, tejas)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not merely a conventional benediction: it states the positive theological premise of the whole work, namely that the Pāñcarātra issues from the supreme four-vyūha Brahman endowed with determinate auspicious qualities. Because the assigned Tattva-Muktā-Kalāpa scan was too OCR-degraded for trustworthy grammatical extraction, this clean Sanskrit opening is an access-constrained but fair way to represent Deśika's positive ontology from the assigned corpus.
key_passages.source_edition: Sri Pancaratra Raksa of Sri Vedanta Desika, critically edited by Pandit M. Duraiswami Aiyangar and Pandit T. Venugopalacharya, Adyar Library, Adyar, Madras, 1942.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
āgamasiddhānto mantrasiddhāntas tantrasiddhāntas tantrāntarasiddhānta iti |
catvāraś cete siddhāntā ṛgādivad eva śākhābhedair avantaratantrair bhidyante ||

pada_analysis.gloss: like the Ṛg and the others

samasa_vigrahas.note: indeclinable comparison: like the Ṛg and the rest

karaka_structure.note: further means or basis of subdivision

key_passages.english_close: Thus: "āgama-siddhānta", "mantra-siddhānta", "tantra-siddhānta", and "tantrāntara-siddhānta". These four are the established doctrinal forms; and just like the Ṛk and the other Vedic [recensions], they are divided into śākhā-distinctions and into subordinate tantras.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (a settled doctrinal formation or established interpretive line)
(a scriptural/ritual manual within the āgamic tradition)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This passage is representative because Deśika is not only defending one text but classifying the whole Pāñcarātra corpus as an internally articulated, Veda-analogous scriptural system. That taxonomic move is central to his epistemology of āgama and to the institutional claim that Śrīvaiṣṇava temple practice rests on a disciplined canon rather than on free sectarian invention.
key_passages.source_edition: Sri Pancaratra Raksa of Sri Vedanta Desika, critically edited by Pandit M. Duraiswami Aiyangar and Pandit T. Venugopalacharya, Adyar Library, Adyar, Madras, 1942.

key_passages.locus_long: Pāñcarātra-Rakṣā, prathamo'dhikāraḥ, section on Vaikhānasa-Pāñcarātra non-conflict
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
avirodhopayogābhyām ubhayor api śāstrayoḥ |
madhyasthair hi kṛto dharmo vidyāsthāna-niveśanam ||

pada_analysis.gloss: the assigning to proper domains of learning

samasa_vigrahas.note: those standing in the middle, i.e. impartial adjudicators
placing each within its proper disciplinary location

karaka_structure.note: genitive relation: the two scriptures whose non-conflict/usefulness is at issue
grounds on which the settlement is made
agent in instrumental with passive kṛtaḥ
subject-predicate core: the norm/settlement has been made

key_passages.english_close: Because of the non-conflict and the [respective] usefulness of both śāstras. A normative settlement (dharma) has indeed been made by impartial authorities — namely, the assigning of each to its proper vidyā-sthāna (domain of learning).

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (here: the normative rule or settlement, not merely religion)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This couplet fairly represents Deśika because his defense of Pāñcarātra is not a rejection of Vedic or Vaikhānasa practice but a carefully delimited account of compatibility, jurisdiction, and ritual propriety. It therefore shows the institutional and devotional side of his thought, where scriptural reasoning issues in ordered worship rather than in a merely abstract canon-theory.
key_passages.source_edition: Sri Pancaratra Raksa of Sri Vedanta Desika, critically edited by Pandit M. Duraiswami Aiyangar and Pandit T. Venugopalacharya, Adyar Library, Adyar, Madras, 1942.
